caption: Fig. 1. (a) Schematic representation of the heat treatment cycles applied to the studied steels, (b) representative methodology for determining the bainitic transformation finish time (Bt) based on dilatometric analysis.
paper_abstract: Institute of Metallurgy, Clausthal University of Technology, 38678, Clausthal-Zellerfeld, Germany
citation_sentences: followed by slow cooling in the oven. Finally, samples are precisely fabricated using a water jet cutting machine (OMAX 2626, Kent, Washington, USA), allowing for accurate geometries and minimizing thermal distortion. Cylindrical specimens $( \cos \mathbf { x } 1 0 \mathbf { m } \mathbf { m } ^ { 3 } )$ ) were subjected to the heat treatments by DIL 805A dilatometer (TA Instruments, New Castle, DE, USA). Tensile tests were carried out using a Zwick/Roell universal testing machine (250 kN load cell, Zwick Roell Group, Ulm, Germany) on ASTM E8 sub-size round specimens (12.5 mm gauge length, 2.5 mm diameter, 7 mm shoulder length). All tensile specimens were heat-treated using the DIL 805A dilatometer, applying the thermal cycle shown in Fig. 1 (a), which includes the transformation finish time (Bt) determined from dilatometric analysis.

The austenitizing temperatures $( \mathrm { T _ { A } } )$ were detected using dilatometry with 1 $\mathrm { K } s ^ { - 1 }$ + heating rate above $1 1 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and MatCalc software (Version: 6.01 (rel 1.003), Institute of Materials Science and Technology, TU Wien, Wien, Austria), based on equilibrium phase calculations with step value calculations. The applied austenitizing temperatures were selected above the calculated and measured values, to ensure a microstructure as free as possible from $\mathfrak { C } _ { \mathfrak { p } ^ { - } }$ and to dissolve all possible carbides. The martensite start temperature (Ms) was determined using dilatometric analysis by rapid cooling at a rate of $1 0 0 ~ \mathrm { K } s ^ { - 1 }$ from the corresponding applied $\mathrm { T _ { A } }$ . Ms on cooling was obtained by a tangentintersection analysis of the dilatometric RLC curves. The results for $\mathrm { T _ { A } }$ and Ms were discussed in the section of 3.1. They were then subjected to heat treatment at austempering temperature $\left( \mathrm { T _ { B } } \right)$ durations determined to be just above the pre-measured Ms temperature (see Table 2) to obtain nB microstructure. Additionally, to facilitate the maximum dissolution of $\textstyle \mathbf { M } _ { \mathbf { X } } \mathbf { C } .$ -type precipitates and release the impingement effect, additional heat treatments at $1 2 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ were conducted on S4 steel. The cooling rate from austenitization to the austempering temperature was 100 $\mathrm { K } s ^ { - 1 }$ , which is sufficiently fast to suppress intermediate phase transformations and ensure fine bainitic transformation (for heat treatments see Fig. 1 (a)).

The transformation finish time (Bt) was defined as the point where the transformation rate decreased to a negligible level, corresponding to the onset of the plateau in the RLC curve. While not mathematically zero, the rate of change beyond this point becomes minimal and consistent with criteria used in prior works $[ 1 5 , 2 2 , 2 3 ]$ . This corresponds to the beginning of the plateau region in the relative length change (RLC (%)) curve shown in Fig. 1 (b)). Although this method involves some subjective interpretation, any resulting variation is expected to have a negligible effect on the final properties [23].

The applied method for $V _ { \gamma }$ determination is summarized in Fig. 2.

caption: Fig. 3. Dilatometric determination of the austenitizing response and LOM verification of polygonal ferrite $( \mathfrak { a } _ { \mathfrak { p } } )$ for steels S1–S5 (a–e). Left column: Heating RLC–T curves (black) with first derivatives d(RLC)/dT (red). The operational $\mathrm { T _ { A } }$ is marked by the vertical dashed line at the temperature where the derivative peak decays and linearity is first restored; for S3 a broadened austenitizing transition is indicated. Middle column: LOM at 1050 ◦C (benchmark), evidencing ${ \mathfrak { X } } _ { \mathrm { p } }$ retention in S1, S3, S4 and S5, while S2 is $\alpha _ { \mathrm { p } } .$ -free at this temperature. Right column: LOM at the selected $\mathrm { T _ { A } }$ (1150 ◦C for S1, S2, S4, S5; 1220 ◦C for S3). $\alpha _ { \mathrm { p } }$ is eliminated in S1 and S2 and reduced to trace levels in S4 and S5, whereas S3 retains a noticeable $\alpha _ { \mathrm { p } }$ fraction even at elevated $\mathrm { T } _ { \mathrm { A } } .$ .
paper_abstract: Institute of Metallurgy, Clausthal University of Technology, 38678, Clausthal-Zellerfeld, Germany
citation_sentences: In this high C–Al alloy set, overlapping carbide-dissolution sequences tend to broaden the RLC–Temperature (T) signal and prevented a single sharp break from marking the austenitizing temperature $\mathrm { T } _ { \mathrm { A } } .$ An operational $\mathrm { T _ { A } }$ was therefore taken from the differentiated curves as the temperature at which the first-derivative peak decayed and linearity of the base curve was first restored. Using this criterion, approximate $\mathrm { T _ { A } }$ values were obtained as $1 0 6 2 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } \left( \mathrm { S } 1 \right)$ , 1031 ◦C (S2), a broadened transition without a unique $\mathrm { T _ { A } }$ for S3, $1 0 6 7 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } \ ( \mathrm { S } 4 )$ , and ${ \sim } 9 0 0 \ { } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ (S5) as shown in Fig. 3. To disentangle concurrent effects and to estimate the carbide-dissolution temperature (Tcd) and the ferrite-free $\mathrm { T _ { A } , }$ MatCalc was employed as an assisting tool (see Fig. 4). The simulations indicated ferrite-free $\mathrm { T _ { A } }$ values of 991 ◦C (S1), 929 ◦C (S2), 1177 ◦C (S3), 1016 ◦C (S4), and 986 ◦C (S5) and predicted complete dissolution of MC-type (Fe, $\mathrm { M ) _ { x } C _ { y } }$ carbides around $1 1 0 3 - 1 1 0 7 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for S1–S2 and near $1 1 3 4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for S4. Guided by these considerations, $1 0 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ was first assessed as a benchmark temperature for all steels to reach the $\alpha _ { \mathrm { p } } .$ -free microstructure (for LOM images see Fig. 3). At 1050 ◦C, S1 displayed a dual-phase structure with ${ \mathfrak { d } } _ { { \mathfrak { p } } } ,$ whereas S2 appeared $\mathfrak { a } _ { \mathfrak { p } ^ { - } } \mathrm { f r e e } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ , consistent with its lower derivative-based $\mathrm { T } _ { \mathrm { A } } .$ S3 exhibited extensive ${ \mathfrak { d } } _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ at $1 0 5 0 ^ { \circ } ($ (area fraction $\sim 1 4 . 6 \% )$ , and ${ \mathfrak { d } } _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ persisted even at $1 2 2 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ (area fraction: \~8.2 %), in line with the simulated requirement ${ \bf o f } \sim 1 1 7 7 ^ { \circ } { \bf C }$ for approaching a ferrite-free state and with the known ferrite-stabilizing effect of very high Al; a single experimental $\mathrm { T _ { A } }$ could therefore not be assigned to S3. S4 contained a visibly higher amount of ${ \mathfrak { d } } _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ at $1 0 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ , but only a trace level (≲ 1 %) at $1 1 5 0 \ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ consistent with the derivative-based $\mathrm { T } _ { \mathrm { A } } .$ Similarly, S5 showed more prominent ${ \mathfrak { d } } _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ at $1 0 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and only a very small residual fraction (≲ 1 %) a $: 1 1 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ while the simulations placed Tcd near ${ \sim } 7 7 5 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and a ferrite-free $\mathrm { T _ { A } }$ around $9 8 6 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ . Taken together, the three data streams (dilatometry, simulation, and LOM) point to $1 1 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ as an optimized austenitizing temperature for S1, S2, S4, and ${ \mathsf { S } } 5 ,$ where carbide dissolution is advanced, chemical homogenization is improved, and ${ \mathfrak { d } } _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ is suppressed to negligible or non-existing levels. For $^ { S 3 , }$ a higher schedule of $1 2 2 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ was required to minimize ${ \mathfrak { X } } _ { \mathrm { p } }$ and to approach the simulated ferrite-free field, although a fully $\alpha _ { \mathrm { p } } .$ -free condition could not be realized within the practical window. Additionally, the rationale for these applied temperatures follows directly from the above synthesis. At substantially lower $\mathrm { T _ { A } , }$ incomplete dissolution and carbon heterogeneity would be retained, which are known to increase scatter in mechanical response and to disturb bainitic transformation through particle-assisted impingement.

caption: Fig. 4. MatCalc equilibrium phase-fraction–temperature diagrams for S1–S5. The maps show bcc (green), fcc (red) and the κ -carbides, MC, hcp, TiC appears only in S4 (blue). Grey boxes mark the ferrite-free $\mathrm { T _ { A } }$ and the carbide-dissolution temperature Tcd indicate dissolution sequences.

Using this criterion, approximate $\mathrm { T _ { A } }$ values were obtained as $1 0 6 2 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } \left( \mathrm { S } 1 \right)$ , 1031 ◦C (S2), a broadened transition without a unique $\mathrm { T _ { A } }$ for S3, $1 0 6 7 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } \ ( \mathrm { S } 4 )$ , and ${ \sim } 9 0 0 \ { } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ (S5) as shown in Fig. 3. The simulations indicated ferrite-free $\mathrm { T _ { A } }$ values of 991 ◦C (S1), 929 ◦C (S2), 1177 ◦C (S3), 1016 ◦C (S4), and 986 ◦C (S5) and predicted complete dissolution of MC-type (Fe, $\mathrm { M ) _ { x } C _ { y } }$ carbides around $1 1 0 3 - 1 1 0 7 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for S1–S2 and near $1 1 3 4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for S4. Guided by these considerations, $1 0 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ was first assessed as a benchmark temperature for all steels to reach the $\alpha _ { \mathrm { p } } .$ -free microstructure (for LOM images see Fig. 3). At 1050 ◦C, S1 displayed a dual-phase structure with ${ \mathfrak { d } } _ { { \mathfrak { p } } } ,$ whereas S2 appeared $\mathfrak { a } _ { \mathfrak { p } ^ { - } } \mathrm { f r e e } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ , consistent with its lower derivative-based $\mathrm { T } _ { \mathrm { A } } .$ S3 exhibited extensive ${ \mathfrak { d } } _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ at $1 0 5 0 ^ { \circ } ($ (area fraction $\sim 1 4 . 6 \% )$ , and ${ \mathfrak { d } } _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ persisted even at $1 2 2 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ (area fraction: \~8.2 %), in line with the simulated requirement ${ \bf o f } \sim 1 1 7 7 ^ { \circ } { \bf C }$ for approaching a ferrite-free state and with the known ferrite-stabilizing effect of very high Al; a single experimental $\mathrm { T _ { A } }$ could therefore not be assigned to S3. S4 contained a visibly higher amount of ${ \mathfrak { d } } _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ at $1 0 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ , but only a trace level (≲ 1 %) at $1 1 5 0 \ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ consistent with the derivative-based $\mathrm { T } _ { \mathrm { A } } .$ Similarly, S5 showed more prominent ${ \mathfrak { d } } _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ at $1 0 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and only a very small residual fraction (≲ 1 %) a $: 1 1 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ while the simulations placed Tcd near ${ \sim } 7 7 5 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and a ferrite-free $\mathrm { T _ { A } }$ around $9 8 6 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ . Taken together, the three data streams (dilatometry, simulation, and LOM) point to $1 1 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ as an optimized austenitizing temperature for S1, S2, S4, and ${ \mathsf { S } } 5 ,$ where carbide dissolution is advanced, chemical homogenization is improved, and ${ \mathfrak { d } } _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ is suppressed to negligible or non-existing levels. For $^ { S 3 , }$ a higher schedule of $1 2 2 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ was required to minimize ${ \mathfrak { X } } _ { \mathrm { p } }$ and to approach the simulated ferrite-free field, although a fully $\alpha _ { \mathrm { p } } .$ -free condition could not be realized within the practical window. Additionally, the rationale for these applied temperatures follows directly from the above synthesis. At substantially lower $\mathrm { T _ { A } , }$ incomplete dissolution and carbon heterogeneity would be retained, which are known to increase scatter in mechanical response and to disturb bainitic transformation through particle-assisted impingement.

In S1–S3, the relatively low Ms values indicate austenite that is effectively carbon-enriched after austenitization; the lowest Ms in S3 is consistent with extended κ-carbide (see Fig. 4 (c)) dissolution together with ferrite retention that facilitates local carbon partitioning into austenite despite the Ms-raising tendency of its very high Al content. In S4, Ms decreased only marginally from $3 1 0 ^ { \circ } \mathbf { C } \left( 1 2 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathbf { C } \right.$ austenitization) to $3 0 5 ^ { \circ } \mathbf { C } \left( 1 1 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathbf { C } \right.$ austenitization). In S4, Ms changes only marginally with austenitizing temperature (305 ◦C at $1 1 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ versus $3 1 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ at $1 2 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ; \Delta \colon 5 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ) ,$ , which is within experimental scatter. No clear effect of the Ti microalloying addition on Ms was evident (Fig. 5 (d)). In S5, the relatively high Ms (331 ◦C) reflects the lower effective carbon in austenite under the chosen condition, in agreement with the continueddissolution signature observed in the heating curve and with its lower overall alloying. Overall, the Ms trends are internally consistent with the alloy chemistries and the austenitization analysis, and they support the heat-treatment strategy: austempering temperatures were chosen slightly above the measured Ms values to promote bainitic transformation while suppressing martensite.

caption: Fig. 6. Bainitic transformation kinetics measured by dilatometry, plotted as relative length change (%) versus time. Figure (a–b) use a logarithmic time axis; subfigures (c–e) replot the same data on a linear time axis to aid readability of transformation times. (a) Al ≥ 6 wt.%: S1–S3. (b) Al < 6 wt.%: S4–S5. (c) Linear-time view of panel (a) for S1 and S2. (d) Linear-time view isolating the slowest transformation from panel (a) for S3 (extended time). (e) Linear-time view of panel (b) for S4 and S5. Vertical dashed lines mark the approximate bainite finish time for each condition.

citation_sentences: Bainitic transformation behavior for the steels with varying C–Al levels was evaluated from the dilatometric RLC (%)–time (s) curves summarized in Fig. 6 (a) and (b). ) plot time on a logarithmic axis, while subfigures (c–e) replot the same data on a linear time axis to make the transformation times easier to read. For the steels S1, S2 and S3 (Al ≥ 6 wt.%), the kinetics are shown in Fig. 6 (a), with the corresponding linear-time views in Fig. 6 (c) and, for the slowest case (S3), an extendedtime view in Fig. 6 (d). For the steels S4 and S5 $( \mathrm { A l } < 6 ~ \mathrm { w t . \% } )$ , the kinetics are shown in Fig. 6 (b) and the linear-time view in Fig. 6 (e). Vertical dashed lines denote the approximate bainite finish time for each condition.

In the study of [46,47], TiMoC exhibits favorable wettability characteristics with the steel matrix in reinforced austenitic high-Mn steels, contributing to enhanced interfacial bonding. Despite a reduction in El, approximately 16 % in the reinforced variant compared to 35 % in conventional steels, the change in UTS and $\mathrm { R } _ { \mathrm { p } 0 . 2 }$ was negligible. These results are intrinsic to the powder metallurgy processing route employed. On the other hand, in our study, the self-nucleated TiMoC complex phase was expected to exhibit superior thermodynamic and structural stability relative to discrete TiC and MoC precipitate systems according to the findings of [48]. Within the S4 steel design framework, incorporation of TiMoC was used to mitigate the formation of $( \mathrm { F e , M ) _ { x } C _ { y } , }$ MoC, and probably κ-carbide phases commonly exists in analogous alloy systems. Additionally, facilitating refined microstructural evolution would help transformation kinetics be accelerated, while the TiMoC architecture further enables controlled coarsening, offering potential improvements in El. These precipitates accelerate bainite nucleation by providing initial nucleation sites for bainite sheaves, leading to a shorter incubation time compared to steels S1, S2, and S3. However, due to the impingement of bainite sheaves (see Fig. 10) caused by an increased number of various phase boundaries [49–51], the progression of bainite transformation after the incubation period was gradually suppressed in S4 compared to S1 and S2 (see Fig. 6).

caption: Fig. 7. Mechanical properties of the investigated steels under various austenitization $\mathrm { ( T _ { A } ) }$ and austempering $\left( \mathrm { { T _ { B } } } \right)$ conditions. UTS (MPa), $\mathrm { R } _ { \mathrm { p 0 } . 2 }$ (MPa), El (%), and hardness (HV30) are presented.
paper_abstract: Institute of Metallurgy, Clausthal University of Technology, 38678, Clausthal-Zellerfeld, Germany
citation_sentences: Mechanical test results of the investigated parameters are given in Fig. 7 and critical points are discussed.

At $\mathrm { { T _ { B } } }$ of 350 ◦C, the standard deviation Lt(t) increased, indicating the presence of bainitic structures with a broader range of thicknesses, such as nB Type 1 and Type 2, alongside blocky γr regions located between them (see Fig. 9). This structural phenomenon is particularly pronounced in the S1, S2, and S3 steels (see Table 3 for the SD difference between the temperatures of $\mathrm { T _ { B } ) }$ . This heterogeneity promotes the formation of interlocked bainitic morphologies consisting of different bainite types. For S4, this correlation is lower, and it is negligible for S5. This indicates that the lower alloying concept suppresses variative Lt(t). This can be considered as an El improving factor as shown in aforementioned Fig. 7.

Nonetheless, the S4 steel exhibited the best average and maximum El of 10 and 12 % at $\mathrm { T _ { A } }$ 1150 ◦C and $\mathrm { T } _ { \mathrm { B } } \colon 3 5 0 \ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ , which are slightly higher than S1and S2 as aforementioned (see Fig. 7). This shows that the suppression of El was not determined due to the existence of TiMoC on the phase and grain boundaries.

caption: Fig. 8. TTP diagrams simulated using MatCalc for the investigated steels: (a) S1 (also approximately representative for S2), (b) S3, (c) S4, (d) S5.
paper_abstract: Institute of Metallurgy, Clausthal University of Technology, 38678, Clausthal-Zellerfeld, Germany
citation_sentences: The TTP diagrams of the S1, S3, S4, and S5 steels are presented in Fig. 8. Due to negligible differences between the S1 and S2 steels, only the S1 steel is shown. $( \mathrm { F e , M ) _ { x } C _ { y } }$ particles precipitate during cooling due to their extremely high nucleation kinetics between approximately 700 ◦C and 1150 ◦C in S1 and S3, and between $7 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and $1 0 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ in S5. As a result, avoiding their formation becomes impossible. In contrast, for S4, initial nucleation occurs rapidly between $7 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and $9 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ but is subsequently suppressed due to the counter-coarsening effect of the Ti–Mo–C precipitate system. Additionally, due to limited diffusivity during austempering, their secondary precipitation requires extremely long durations and stays constant. TTP curves also indicated a slight increase in $( \mathrm { F e , M ) _ { x } C _ { y } }$ in S3 compared to S1 and S2. However, this minor stoichiometric shift in the TTP curve nose, attributed to the higher Al content, appears to have a negligible impact on the mechanical properties.

As expected, $( \mathrm { F e , M ) _ { x } C _ { y } }$ and MoC precipitates were less present in the final microstructure compared to steels S1, S2 and $^ { S 3 , }$ due to the suppresing effect of TiMoC. The longitudinal alignment of TiMoC precipitates indicates their formation during the cooling phase after casting, followed by deformation during subsequent hot forming, indicating that secondary precipitation played a negligible role (Fig. 10 (a), (b), (c)). These findings are compatible with the results of TTP (see Fig. 8 (c)). To achieve the desired microstructure through the precipitation of Ti–Mo carbides along with a controlled amount of free TiC, an austenitization temperature of $1 2 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ was selected. This temperature was also specifically chosen because it suppressed partially the impingement associated with the extremely small prior austenite grain sizes observed at 1150 ${ } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ thereby facilitating relatively faster phase transformations. Furthermore, a primary characteristic of Ti–Mo–C complex carbides is the absence of micro-defects within the microstructure (Fig. 10 (a), (b), (c)). As illustrated by the EDX mapping, a depletion of Fe was detected within the complex carbides, further confirming their composition (see Fig. 10 (d)).

In S5, the moderate Al–C level together with a small Ni addition promotes very rapid bainitic transformation when austempered slightly above Ms, yielding a predominantly bainitic microstructure with filmlike γr and only isolated secondary constituents (see Fig. 8 (d)). The EBSD maps substantiate this fast kinetics by revealing a fine, wellconnected bainitic ferrite network with minimal impingement. The IPF maps (Fig. 11 (d), (e)) for $\mathrm { { T _ { B } } }$ at $3 3 5 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ show narrow packets of bainitic ferrite laths with small, scattered lenticular martensite (labelled M). The packets exhibit intra-packet orientation clustering, as expected for laths sharing a parent variant, separated by frequent highangle packet/block boundaries. The corresponding KAM maps (Fig. 11 (g), (h)) display bands of elevated local misorientation along lath/ packet boundaries and at sheaf tips; in the martensite-focused field (Fig. 11 (h)) local KAM hot-spots occur adjacent to M, consistent with the high density of geometrically necessary dislocations generated by low-temperature displacive growth. Although dilatometry gave Ms of 331 ◦C, the sporadic M plates at 335 ◦C (Ms+4 K) are compatible with stress-assisted transformation and local thermal/compositional heterogeneities, i.e. Ms is distributed at the microscale. XRD at this condition indicates the highest micro-strain in austenite (εγ: 0.51 %), slightly above ferrite (εα: 0.45 %), and a lower retained-austenite fraction (19 %) (Table 3). These features explain the higher strength but reduced ductility measured at 335 ◦C (UTS: 1530 MPa, El: 7 %): finer plates and concentrated defect fields raise strength, while the lower γr content limits TRIP-assisted strain hardening.

caption: Fig. 10. SEM and EDX analysis of steel S4 after different heat treatment conditions: (a) SEM for S4, $\mathrm { T } _ { \mathrm { A } } \colon 1 2 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ TB: 350 ◦C (b) SEM for S4, TA: 1150 ◦C, TB: 350 ◦C (c) SEM for S4, $\mathrm { T _ { A } } \mathrm { : }$ 1150 ◦C, T : 320 ◦C (d) EDX mapping of (c).

The longitudinal alignment of TiMoC precipitates indicates their formation during the cooling phase after casting, followed by deformation during subsequent hot forming, indicating that secondary precipitation played a negligible role (Fig. 10 (a), (b), (c)). These findings are compatible with the results of TTP (see Fig. 8 (c)). To achieve the desired microstructure through the precipitation of Ti–Mo carbides along with a controlled amount of free TiC, an austenitization temperature of $1 2 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ was selected. This temperature was also specifically chosen because it suppressed partially the impingement associated with the extremely small prior austenite grain sizes observed at 1150 ${ } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ thereby facilitating relatively faster phase transformations. Furthermore, a primary characteristic of Ti–Mo–C complex carbides is the absence of micro-defects within the microstructure (Fig. 10 (a), (b), (c)). As illustrated by the EDX mapping, a depletion of Fe was detected within the complex carbides, further confirming their composition (see Fig. 10 (d)).

caption: Fig. 11. S5 microstructure analysis: (a) SEM for TB: 335 ◦C, (b) SEM for TB: 365 ◦C, (c) XRD for TB: 335 ◦C shows α+γr with no resolvable martensite. EBSD IPF (d–f) and KAM (g–i): 335 ◦C (d, e, g, h) → fine bainite with local KAM spikes near M; 365 ◦C (f, i) → slightly coarser bainite with trace F and marginally higher global KAM.
paper_abstract: Institute of Metallurgy, Clausthal University of Technology, 38678, Clausthal-Zellerfeld, Germany
citation_sentences: In S5, the moderate Al–C level together with a small Ni addition promotes very rapid bainitic transformation when austempered slightly above Ms, yielding a predominantly bainitic microstructure with filmlike γr and only isolated secondary constituents (see Fig. 8 (d)). The EBSD maps substantiate this fast kinetics by revealing a fine, wellconnected bainitic ferrite network with minimal impingement. The IPF maps (Fig. 11 (d), (e)) for $\mathrm { { T _ { B } } }$ at $3 3 5 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ show narrow packets of bainitic ferrite laths with small, scattered lenticular martensite (labelled M). The packets exhibit intra-packet orientation clustering, as expected for laths sharing a parent variant, separated by frequent highangle packet/block boundaries. The corresponding KAM maps (Fig. 11 (g), (h)) display bands of elevated local misorientation along lath/ packet boundaries and at sheaf tips; in the martensite-focused field (Fig. 11 (h)) local KAM hot-spots occur adjacent to M, consistent with the high density of geometrically necessary dislocations generated by low-temperature displacive growth. Although dilatometry gave Ms of 331 ◦C, the sporadic M plates at 335 ◦C (Ms+4 K) are compatible with stress-assisted transformation and local thermal/compositional heterogeneities, i.e. Ms is distributed at the microscale. XRD at this condition indicates the highest micro-strain in austenite (εγ: 0.51 %), slightly above ferrite (εα: 0.45 %), and a lower retained-austenite fraction (19 %) (Table 3). These features explain the higher strength but reduced ductility measured at 335 ◦C (UTS: 1530 MPa, El: 7 %): finer plates and concentrated defect fields raise strength, while the lower γr content limits TRIP-assisted strain hardening.

The IPF map (Fig. 11 (f)) for at $\mathrm { { T _ { B } } }$ of $3 6 5 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ reveals slightly coarser bainitic packets and a trace of allotriomorphic ferrite (labelled F), while martensite is absent. The global KAM field (Fig. 11 (i)) is marginally higher on average than at 335 ◦C (mean KAM \~ 0.905◦ vs 0.825◦; median 0.789◦ vs 0.692), whereas local KAM maxima are more pronounced near M at 335 ◦C. KAM is step-size sensitive; the maps were acquired at different magnifications $( 2 5 \mathbf { k } \times \mathbf { v s } 2 0 \mathbf { k } \times \mathbf { ) } .$ , so small differences in absolute values should be interpreted with this measurement sensitivity in mind. XRD corroborates the microstructural evolution. The retainedaustenite fraction increases to \~26 % and the phase micro-strains converge (εγ: 0.42 %, εα: 0.43 %), consistent with reduced lattice curvature at the higher transformation temperature and improved deformation uniformity. The mechanical response follows accordingly (UTS: 1315 MPa, El: 8.7 %).

Lenticular martensite is only sporadically observed by SEM/EBSD at 335 ${ } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ and the XRD pattern shows no resolvable martensite peaks (see Fig. 11 (c)), implying a volume fraction below the diffractometric detectability under the present conditions [52]. γr is largely film-like at bainite lath boundaries; because these films are near or below the EBSD step size, EBSD under-counts $\gamma \mathbf { r }$ relative to XRD, hence XRD is taken as quantitative for phase fractions. In contrast to the higher-Al grades, S5 shows no SEM-resolvable $( \mathrm { F e , M ) _ { x } C _ { y } }$ after austempering, in line with the TTP assessment and the lower alloying level; the relatively symmetric XRD peak shapes further indicate limited precipitation-induced broadening.
